No Poverty.reason: The course explicitly addresses 'precarity,' 'class differentiation,' and 'social inequality' among migrant populations, directly linking to poverty reduction (Target 1.2) and ensuring economic resources for the vulnerable (Target 1.4).

Zero Hunger.reason: No evidence related to hunger, food security, agriculture, or malnutrition was found in the course syllabus.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course discusses 'care' and 'affective labor' within transnational families, which can be inferred to relate to aspects of mental health and well-being (Target 3.4), though not direct health services.

Quality Education.reason: As a graduate-level course, it provides tertiary education (Target 4.3) and aims to enhance students' awareness of social inequality and digital divides, contributing to education for sustainable development (Target 4.7).
evidence: Enhance students’ awareness of social inequality, class gap and digital divide

Gender Equality.reason: The course explicitly focuses on 'gender politics,' 'misogyny,' and the experiences of 'Rural Migrant Women,' directly addressing discrimination (Target 5.1) and promoting gender equality (Target 5.c).

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No evidence related to water quality, sanitation, hygiene, or water resource management was found in the course syllabus.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No evidence related to energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency was found in the course syllabus.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course's core themes are 'labor,' 'digital labor,' 'gig economy,' and 'precarity,' directly addressing decent work (Target 8.5) and protecting migrant workers in precarious employment (Target 8.8).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course focuses on the 'platform economy,' 'digital media era,' 'internet, smartphones,' and their impacts on labor, aligning with enhancing technological capabilities (Target 9.b) and ICT access (Target 9.c).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: A central objective is to analyze 'social inequality, class gap and digital divide,' directly addressing the reduction of inequalities (Target 10.2) and facilitating responsible migration policies (Target 10.7).
evidence: social inequality, class gap and digital divide

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The course examines 'rural-to-urban migration' and 'return migration,' which are key aspects of urbanization and the dynamics between rural and urban areas (Target 11.a).

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: No evidence related to sustainable consumption, waste reduction, or resource efficiency was found in the course syllabus.

Climate Action.reason: No evidence related to climate change, its impacts, or mitigation/adaptation strategies was found in the course syllabus.

Life Below Water.reason: No evidence related to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fisheries was found in the course syllabus.

Life on Land.reason: No evidence related to terrestrial ecosystems, biodiversity, forests, or land degradation was found in the course syllabus.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course discusses 'exploitation' and 'misogyny in China’s State-Market Nexus,' which tangentially relates to promoting non-discriminatory policies (Target 16.b) and reducing structural violence (Target 16.1).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: As an 'International Master's Program' focusing on 'transnational migration' and cases from 'East Asian and Southeast Asian countries,' the course inherently fosters international knowledge sharing and cooperation (Target 17.6).
evidence: East Asian and Southeast Asian countries
